perturbation.original_text: TO THE FULLEST EXTENT PERMITTED BY APPLICABLE LAW EACH PARTY HEREBY IRREVOCABLY WAIVES ALL RIGHT OF TRIAL BY JURY IN ANY ACTION, PROCEEDING, CLAIM, OR COUNTERCLAIM ARISING OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THIS AGREEMENT OR ANY MATTER ARISING HEREUNDER.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee expressly recognizes and acknowledges that its use of the Licensed Mark shall inure solely to the benefit of Licensor, and shall not confer on Licensee any ownership rights to the Licensed Mark. Licensee agrees and covenants that it shall not challenge, contest, or take any actions inconsistent with Licensor's exclusive rights of ownership of the Licensed Mark. TO THE FULLEST EXTENT PERMITTED BY APPLICABLE LAW EACH PARTY HEREBY IRREVOCABLY WAIVES ALL RIGHT OF TRIAL BY JURY IN ANY ACTION, PROCEEDING, CLAIM, OR COUNTERCLAIM ARISING OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THIS AGREEMENT OR ANY MATTER ARISING HEREUNDER.
perturbation.explanation: The jury trial waiver is moved to the 'Goodwill' section, where it is less likely to be noticed by the Licensee, potentially hindering their awareness of this critical clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 7th Amendment to the United States Constitution
perturbation.law_explanation: The Seventh Amendment guarantees the right to a jury trial in civil cases. While a jury trial waiver is permissible, its placement in an obscure section like 'Goodwill' could be argued as undermining the knowing and voluntary nature of the waiver, potentially conflicting with the constitutional right to a jury trial.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Seventh Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Seventh Amendment The Seventh Amendment continues a practice from English common law of distinguishing civil claims which must be tried before a jury (absent waiver by the parties) from claims and issues that may be heard by a judge alone. It only governs federal civil courts and has no application to civil courts set up by the states when those courts are hearing only disputes of state law . Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment VII In suits at common law, where the value in controversy shall exceed twenty dollars, the right of trial by jury shall be preserved, and no fact tried by a jury, shall be otherwise reexamined in any court of the United States, than according to the rules of the common law. Wex Resources Criminal Law Criminal Procedure Jury Other Resources US Code: Title 18 Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure ‹ Sixth Amendment up Eighth Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The modified contract moves the jury trial waiver to the 'Goodwill' section, potentially hindering the Licensee's awareness of it. The Seventh Amendment preserves the right to a jury trial, but this right can be waived. However, the Law Explanation argues that placing the waiver in an obscure section could undermine the knowing and voluntary nature of the waiver. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the Seventh Amendment distinguishes civil claims which must be tried before a jury (absent waiver by the parties). Therefore, by obscuring the waiver, the modified text creates a structural flaw that contradicts the requirement for a knowing waiver, affecting the enforceability of the waiver and potentially violating the Seventh Amendment.

perturbation.original_text: 1.3 Trademark Notices. All print and electronic displays of the Licensed Mark by Licensee shall include at Licensor's option, a notice to the effect that the Licensed Mark are owned by Licensor and used by Licensee under license from Licensor.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.8 Headings. The headings contained in this Agreement are for convenience of reference only and shall not be considered in construing this Agreement. 1.3 Trademark Notices. All print and electronic displays of the Licensed Mark by Licensee shall include at Licensor's option, a notice to the effect that the Licensed Mark are owned by Licensor and used by Licensee under license from Licensor.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'Trademark Notices' requirement into the 'Headings' section makes it appear as though the notice is non-binding or merely a suggestion, undermining the importance of adhering to trademark ownership information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Lanham Act protects trademark holders from infringement. By placing the trademark notice requirement in an unrelated section (Headings) it weakens the enforcement of trademark rights because it can easily be missed/misinterpreted by licensees, making it difficult for licensors to prove that licensees were properly informed of ownership and usage guidelines.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a structural flaw by placing the trademark notice requirement within the 'Headings' section, which, according to the perturbation explanation, undermines its importance. The law explanation states this weakens trademark rights enforcement. Scraped Law Snippet 2, particularly section 1111, emphasizes the 'Notice of registration; display with mark; recovery of profits and damages in infringement suit,' suggesting a clear intention that trademark notices be prominently displayed and understood. Moving the notice requirement to a less relevant section contradicts this intent by potentially obscuring the notice and weakening its legal effect. This aligns with the definition of structural flaws where required disclosures are placed in misleading sections, leading to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Licensee shall defend, indemnify and hold harmless Licensor and its officers, directors, employees, agents, corporate subsidiaries, parents, and affiliates ("Licensor Indemnitees") from and against any and all demands, claims, actions or causes of action, assessments, deficiencies, damages, losses, liabilities and expenses (including, without limitation, reasonable expenses of investigation and attorneys' fees and expenses), incurred in conjunction with or arising out of or relating to any third-party claim concerning the Licensed Products and any acts or omissions of Licensee with respect to the Licensed Mark, including without limitation Licensee's performance of its obligations under this Agreement. The Licensor Indemnitees agree to cooperate with Licensee, at Licensee's expense, to provide copies of any documents or materials reasonably requested by Licensee in support of its defense of the Licensor Indemnitees.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee agrees to comply with Licensor's trademark usage guidelines and any other policies and requirements applicable to the Licensed Mark. Licensee shall defend, indemnify and hold harmless Licensor and its officers, directors, employees, agents, corporate subsidiaries, parents, and affiliates ("Licensor Indemnitees") from and against any and all demands, claims, actions or causes of action, assessments, deficiencies, damages, losses, liabilities and expenses (including, without limitation, reasonable expenses of investigation and attorneys' fees and expenses), incurred in conjunction with or arising out of or relating to any third-party claim concerning the Licensed Products and any acts or omissions of Licensee with respect to the Licensed Mark, including without limitation Licensee's performance of its obligations under this Agreement. The Licensor Indemnitees agree to cooperate with Licensee, at Licensee's expense, to provide copies of any documents or materials reasonably requested by Licensee in support of its defense of the Licensor Indemnitees.
perturbation.explanation: The 'Indemnification' clause is moved into the section concerning compliance with trademark usage guidelines. This obscures the broad scope of the indemnification, making it appear as though it only applies to trademark-related issues rather than any third-party claim concerning the licensed products.
perturbation.law_explanation: Indemnification clauses are typically interpreted strictly against the indemnitee (the party being protected). By placing the indemnification clause under the guise of 'trademark usage guidelines compliance,' it misrepresents the full scope of liability, potentially making it harder to enforce the full extent of protection intended by the original clause and creating an unfair burden on the licensee beyond just trademark usage.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: indemnity | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you indemnity Indemnity is a type of insurance that covers a wide range of damages and losses . In the indemnity clause, one party commits to compensate another party for any prospective loss or damage. More common is in insurance contracts, in exchange for premiums paid by the insured to the insurer , the insurer offers to compensate the insured for any potential damages or losses. Depending on the clause of the indemnity agreement , indemnity may be paid in cash or in the form of repairs or replacement. Indemnity also refers to legal exemption from penalties attaching to unconstitutional or illegal actions, typically granted to public officers. [Last reviewed in April of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities insurance accidents & injuries (tort law) tort damages THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics business organizations contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions courts and procedure criminal law and procedure legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction' perturbation type indicates a problem with the contract's organization affecting clarity and enforceability. Specifically, the indemnification clause, a critical protection for the licensor, has been moved into a section concerning trademark usage, obscuring its broad application to all third-party claims related to licensed products, not just trademark issues. The Law Explanation confirms that indemnification clauses are interpreted strictly against the indemnitee. Placing the clause under 'trademark usage guidelines compliance' misrepresents the clause's scope, potentially making it harder to enforce the full intended protection. The Wex snippet defines indemnity as 'one party commits to compensate another party for any prospective loss or damage.' By obscuring the indemnification clause's true scope, the changed text weakens the licensor's protection against losses and damages, contradicting the purpose of an indemnification clause. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.
